fr:mainmatter.html:p: I just untangled a knot of about 25 necklaces. (I've just moved house, and all my necklaces got twisted and tangled together in the move.) It took about half an hour, maybe a bit more. It started because I was getting dressed for the day, and I wanted one necklace in particular: I had a goal in mind. But it became apparent after about a minute or two that going after the necklace I wanted was going nowhere: it was too tangled up with the others, which were too tangled up with each other in turn. I was not going to get the necklace I was after without a much larger-scale untangling.

So I figure, fine. I need to untangle these all to put them away at some point, and that's a job I'd been putting off. No need to do the whole thing now, but if I can get five or six necklaces free, including the one I'm after, that puts a dent in it, and leaves me less to do later. So I start untangling.

In the course of the next half hour or so, I ended up untangling the entire mess. Along the way, I was struck by a number of parallels between the untangling I was doing and the process of solving a logic puzzle. Here's what I can recall immediately afterwards:
fr:mainmatter.html:ul:
For most of the time, the goal I'm after (the thing to be proved or discovered, the necklace I intend to wear today) is not---and cannot be---at the forefront of my mind. Instead, I'm thinking mainly about *what I can do from where I am*: which necklace I see wrapped around which other necklace in a way where I can clarify things by taking a step. Then I take the step, even if I don't see how it connects to the overall project of untangling.
For most of those steps taken along the way, I *never* come to see how they contribute; I just do them and forget them immediately, working directly with the new simpler tangle.
Probably a number of those steps in fact made things *worse* overall in ways I don't see; it would be kind of a miracle if this wasn't the case. But overall *enough* of them make it *enough* better to be worth it.
Even though I'm acting *kind of* at random, it's definitely not *totally* at random. Picking up the tangle and shaking it, for example, is not something I ever do. (Even though the process is frustrating and it would feel good.) I know that doing *that* would make things worse, so I avoid it.
I'm put in mind of a particular student from last semester, who would get frustrated (of course!) at times, but never once gave up. I try to bring her energy to the present task.
Cheap answers and shortcuts are great when they work---but they're not the kind of thing to do without a plan. Sometimes I can take a "shortcut" by opening a necklace's clasp, freeing up two ends that I can then snake out individually. But I think that just opening up all the clasps at once at the start would leave me with an even worse mess. So I don't open up any clasps until I can see directly that opening *this* clasp *now* will let me free a particular necklace. When I see an opportunity to do that, though, I take it right away. Cheap answers and shortcuts are great when they work---but they're not the kind of thing to do without a plan.
Along the way, there are a few times where I just find a necklace sitting on the ground: things I'd done to other necklaces had freed it, but I hadn't noticed when it happened, only later.
The necklace I started out intending to free was in fact like the third one from the end to get free. Sometimes solving the particular problem in view requires solving a much larger and more general problem first.
About two-thirds of the way through, I release a different necklace from the one I'd initially wanted to wear today, and I decide to wear that one instead. Then I keep going.
I never do develop any real understanding of the tangle I began with. The first time I get anything like an overview of the situation, I'm already almost done: like five or so necklaces left. Up until then, it stays too complex to have an overview. Understanding comes *late*: it arrives, if it ever does, when you're almost done.
